title: Post-Call Processing & Webhooks
lede: Every call your AI agent takes is full of valuable data — who called, what they wanted, and whether the goal was met. The Webhooks Post Processing panel turns each finished call into a summary, a success score, and clean structured JSON, then POSTs it all to any system you choose.

steps.title: Open Your Agent
steps.content:
Post-call processing is configured per agent, so start by opening the agent you want to work on.

  1. In the left sidebar, under Intelligence Center, click Smart Agents.
  2. In the agents list, click the name of the agent you want to configure (for example Agent_9278647). The agent editor opens.
💡 If your editor opens in the visual Node view, click Form in the top toolbar — this guide follows the Form view.

images.alt: Smart Agents list under Intelligence Center
images.caption: Intelligence Center → Smart Agents — click an agent's name to open its editor.

images.alt: Smart Agents list under Intelligence Center
images.caption: Intelligence Center → Smart Agents — click an agent's name to open its editor.

steps.title: Find the Webhooks Post Processing Row
steps.content:
The middle column of the agent editor lists every capability your agent has — Knowledge Base, Voice, Brains, and more.

  1. Locate the Webhooks Post Processing row in that list.
  2. Note the ON / OFF pill on the right edge of the row — it lights up green once at least one post-call event is enabled, so you can see at a glance whether this agent reports its calls.
  3. Click anywhere on the Webhooks Post Processing row to open its configuration window.

images.alt: Agent capability list with the Webhooks Post Processing row
images.caption: The agent's capability list — click the Webhooks Post Processing row to open the panel.

images.alt: Agent capability list with the Webhooks Post Processing row
images.caption: The agent's capability list — click the Webhooks Post Processing row to open the panel.

steps.title: Tour the Panel
steps.content:
The Webhooks Post Processing window is split in two: a section list on the left and the active section's settings on the right.

  • Server URL & Events — where to POST call data and which events to send.
  • Summary — the prompt that writes a short recap of each call.
  • Success Evaluation — decides whether the call met its goal, on a rubric you pick.
  • Structured Data — a custom JSON schema extracted from the transcript.

Above the sections sits the Webhook Model row, which applies to all of them. Click any section on the left to work on it, and click Done when you finish.

images.caption: Four sections on the left, settings on the right, and the shared Webhook Model on top.
Four sections on the left, settings on the right, and the shared Webhook Model on top.

steps.title: Pick the Webhook Model
steps.content:
After each call, an LLM reads the transcript and produces your summary, success score, and structured data. You control which model does that work.

  1. In the Webhook Model row at the top, pick a model from the dropdown — or leave it empty to use the platform default (gpt-5-mini).
  2. If you pick an OpenAI reasoning model (gpt-5.x or o-series), the Reasoning Effort dropdown unlocks: choose None (use plugin default), Low, Medium, or High. Other providers simply ignore this setting.
💡 The default model is fast and inexpensive — only switch if you need deeper reasoning for complex extractions.

images.alt: Webhook Model and Reasoning Effort selectors
images.caption: One model powers the summary, evaluation, and structured-data extraction.

images.alt: Webhook Model and Reasoning Effort selectors
images.caption: One model powers the summary, evaluation, and structured-data extraction.

steps.title: Set the Server URL and Choose Events
steps.content:
This is the delivery address for your call data. iMash sends it as HTTP POST requests the moment each call ends.

  1. Open the Server URL & Events section.
  2. In Server URL, paste the endpoint that should receive the data (your backend, a Zapier/Make hook, or an iMash Automation webhook — see the last step).
  3. Under Enabled Settings → Webhook Events, tick each event you want delivered:
  • Summary — the call recap written by your Summary prompt.
  • Success Evaluation — the pass/fail or score result.
  • Structured Data — the extracted JSON fields.
  • Transcript — the full call transcript.
  • Status Updates — real-time events such as call started and call ended.
  • Tool Calls — details of any tools the agent used during the conversation.
⚠️ Ticking an event without a Server URL means nothing is delivered externally — but summaries, evaluations, and structured data are still generated and saved on the call's log entry, so you can enable them even without an endpoint.

images.alt: Server URL field and the six webhook event checkboxes
images.caption: Paste your endpoint, then tick exactly the events you want POSTed.

images.alt: Server URL field and the six webhook event checkboxes
images.caption: Paste your endpoint, then tick exactly the events you want POSTed.

steps.title: Write the Summary Prompt
steps.content:
Never re-listen to a call again — the summary gives you the whole conversation in two or three sentences.

  1. Click Summary in the left section list.
  2. In Prompt, describe how each call should be summarized. A solid starting point: "You are an expert note-taker. You will be given a transcript of a call. Summarize the call in 2-3 sentences, if applicable."

The result is stored with the call and shown on the Call Logs page, so your team sees the gist of every conversation without opening a transcript.

images.caption: Tell the model how to summarize — short and factual works best.
Tell the model how to summarize — short and factual works best.

steps.title: Score Every Call with Success Evaluation
steps.content:
Did the agent actually book the appointment? Close the sale? Success Evaluation answers that automatically, for every single call.

  1. Click Success Evaluation in the left section list.
  2. In Prompt, describe what "success" means for this agent — for example: "You are an expert call evaluator. Determine if the call was successful based on whether the caller booked an appointment." If you leave it empty, the model infers the goal from the agent's own system prompt.

You can use the prompt alone, the rubric alone (next step), or both together — when both are provided they are combined into one set of instructions.

images.caption: Define success in plain language; the model scores each call against it.
Define success in plain language; the model scores each call against it.

steps.title: Choose a Scoring Rubric
steps.content:
The rubric locks the evaluation into a consistent format, so scores are comparable across thousands of calls.

  1. Below the prompt, open the Success Evaluation Rubric dropdown.
  2. Pick the scale that fits your reporting:
  • A scale of 1 to 10 — a simple numeric score.
  • A scale of Excellent, Good, Fair, Poor — descriptive grades.
  • A checklist of criteria and their status — item-by-item pass marks.
  • A grid that evaluates multiple criteria across different performance levels — a full scoring matrix.
  • A scale of 0% to 100% — percentage scoring.
  • A scale of Strongly Agree … Strongly Disagree — Likert-style rating.
  • Automatically break down evaluation into several criteria — the model invents sensible criteria and scores each.
  • A simple 'true' if call passed, 'false' if not — clean pass/fail, perfect for dashboards and automations.
💡 Starting out? Pick pass/fail — it is the easiest result to filter on and to branch an automation with.

images.alt: Success Evaluation Rubric dropdown with all scale options
images.caption: Eight rubric styles — from a 1-10 scale to a simple pass/fail.

images.alt: Success Evaluation Rubric dropdown with all scale options
images.caption: Eight rubric styles — from a 1-10 scale to a simple pass/fail.

steps.title: Extract Structured Data
steps.content:
This is where post-processing gets really powerful: define the exact fields you care about, and the model fills them in from the transcript — clean JSON, every call.

  1. Click Structured Data in the left section list.
  2. (Optional) In Prompt, give extraction guidance — e.g. "Extract the caller's details exactly as stated; leave fields empty if not mentioned."
  3. Under Data Schema, click + Add Property for each field you want.
  4. For every property, fill in:
    • Property Name — the JSON key, e.g. customer_name.
    • Description — tell the model what to capture, e.g. "The caller's full name, exactly as they said it."
    • Type — string, number, boolean, object (nests its own properties), or array (you define the item type).
  5. Delete a property anytime with the red trash button next to it.
💡 Write descriptions like instructions to a new assistant — the clearer the description, the more accurate the extraction. You can use the schema alone or combine it with the prompt; both are merged into one instruction set.

images.alt: Structured Data prompt and Data Schema builder with a customer_name property
images.caption: Each property = one JSON field: name, description, and type.

images.alt: Structured Data prompt and Data Schema builder with a customer_name property
images.caption: Each property = one JSON field: name, description, and type.

steps.title: Pipe Results into an Automation
steps.content:
The fastest way to act on call data — no code required — is to point the webhook at an iMash Automation.

  1. In the left sidebar, click Automation (under Intelligence Center) and create a new flow.
  2. Start the flow with a Catch Webhook trigger and copy the URL it generates.
  3. Back in your agent's Webhooks Post Processing → Server URL & Events, paste that URL as the Server URL and tick the events you need.
  4. Click Done, run a test call, and watch the flow fire — email the summary to your team, log structured data to a sheet, or branch on a failed success evaluation to trigger a follow-up.

That's the full loop: your agent talks, iMash analyzes, and your systems react — automatically, on every call.

support.title: Need Help?
support.content:
If you run into any issues or have questions:

  • 📧 Email us at support@imash.io
  • 💬 Join our Discord community
  • 📚 Check out our API Reference
  • 🎥 Watch our video tutorials

metaDescription: Configure iMash Webhooks Post Processing step by step: pick the webhook model, set a Server URL, choose events, write summary and success-evaluation prompts, build a structured-data schema, and pipe results into automations.
metaKeywords: webhooks, post processing, imash, smart agent, structured data, success evaluation, call summary, webhook model, data schema, automation
